coredata.dc:title: From waste to fuel: Metal-free carbon nanodots for selective CO2 photoreduction into methanol
coredata.dc:creator: Bressi, Viviana
Len, Thomas
Abate, Salvatore
Espro, Claudia
Luque, Rafael
coredata.dc:description: Addressing the major challenge of global warming by implementing a circular carbon cycle involving CO2 conversion is currently an urgent priority for a more sustainable future. The design of efficient, stable, cheap and eco-friendly systems for such purpose is a remarkable challenge. This study reports an unprecedented metal-free, co-catalyst-free orange peel waste–derived carbon nanodot highly active and selective system for the photoreduction of CO2 into methanol. The waste-derived photocatalyst exhibited a maximum methanol production rate of 416.6 µmolMeOH.gcat -1.h−1 and a highly stable methanol production rate of 12.9 µmolMeOH.gcat -1.h−1 after 72 h. This work proposes a successful waste-to-fuel strategy that combines the valorization of orange peel waste and CO2 conversion for the synthesis of green methanol.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanodots
Metal-free
CO2 photoreduction
Waste-to-fuel

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: VB would like to express her gratitude to the University of Messina and the University of Cordoba for the opportunity to collaborate, as well as to Marzia for her unwavering support. TL gratefully acknowledges Ministerio de Ciencia e Innovacion for the provision of a Juan de la Cierva Fellowship (Ref: FJC2021-047151-I).

VB would like to express her gratitude to the University of Messina and the University of Cordoba for the opportunity to collaborate, as well as to Marzia for her unwavering support. TL gratefully acknowledges Ministerio de Ciencia e Innovacion for the provision of a Juan de la Cierva Fellowship (Ref: FJC2021-047151-I).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM micrograph of carbon nanodots at different magnification. Size histogram of 96 different carbon dots.
XPS spectra for C1s (a) N1s (b) O1s (c) and Survey (d) for CDs.
A: Raman spectrum of carbon nanodots. B: FTIR spectrum of carbon nanodots. The numbers in red correspond to the Raman shift and FTIR bands’ wavenumber, respectively.

UV–Vis absorbance and photoluminescence of carbon nanodots. The bottom-right insert is a photo of the sample during photoluminescence experiment. The top-right insert is the Tauc plot for the determination of indirect band gap.
A: Methanol production during CO2 photoreduction using carbon nanodots. B: Cumulative methanol hourly production during CO2 photoreduction using carbon nanodots.
Proposed mechanism for the CO2 photoreduction into methanol using carbon nanodots.

head.ce:title: From waste to fuel: Metal-free carbon nanodots for selective CO2 photoreduction into methanol

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Addressing the major challenge of global warming by implementing a circular carbon cycle involving CO2 conversion is currently an urgent priority for a more sustainable future. The design of efficient, stable, cheap and eco-friendly systems for such purpose is a remarkable challenge. This study reports an unprecedented metal-free, co-catalyst-free orange peel waste–derived carbon nanodot highly active and selective system for the photoreduction of CO2 into methanol. The waste-derived photocatalyst exhibited a maximum methanol production rate of 416.6 µmolMeOH.gcat -1.h−1 and a highly stable methanol production rate of 12.9 µmolMeOH.gcat -1.h−1 after 72 h. This work proposes a successful waste-to-fuel strategy that combines the valorization of orange peel waste and CO2 conversion for the synthesis of green methanol.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanodots (CNDs) are zero-dimensional nanoparticles mainly composed of carbon and with a size ranging from some nanometers to several dozens of nanometers [1]. These CNDs can be obtained from different sources including waste [2,3], synthetic precursors [4] and biomass [5]. Synthetic chemical and physical methodologies for CNDs are typically categorized into two main methods: top-down and bottom-up. In top-down approaches i.e. laser ablation, oxidative cleavage, electrochemical oxidation, large carbon-based materials are broken down into smaller units by physical/chemical forces. In bottom-up approaches, molecular precursors are assembled from smaller carbon units, e.g., carbohydrates, polymers, natural products [6], and biomass [7]. This assembly process can be achieved by several techniques, including hydrothermal carbonization, microwave irradiation, pyrolysis, solvothermal and thermal decomposition methods. The versatility of CNDs sources and preparation methods lead to materials with tailored properties such as water solubility [8], desirable fluorescent behavior [9], ease of functionalization [10,11], low toxicity, and biocompatibility [12], which allowed a wide range of potential applications including drug delivery [13], photocatalysis [14–17], bioimaging [18], sensing [19–21] and water purification [22]. The emerging photocatalytic behaviors of these nanomaterials have only been recently investigated [23–27] most importantly including a recent highlight from our group [14] which demonstrated for the first time the potential of these eco-friendly carbon nanodots in selective photoconversions.

Carbon dioxide concentration in the atmosphere has remarkably raised to over 400 ppm in the early 21st century. Over methane, volatile organic compounds, halogenated gases or nitrous oxide, CO2 is the main contributor to the greenhouse effect [28]. Taking that into account, the worldwide scientific community has made growing efforts to develop technologies capable of capturing, storing or converting CO2. In addition to decreasing carbon dioxide concentration, its conversion into value-added chemicals such as methanol or higher alcohols could create attractive opportunities (e.g. additional revenues for companies) in addition to greener alternatives for energy vectors and chemicals production.

Hydrogenation [29], electroreduction [30,31] as well as photoreduction [32,33] of CO2 are well known pathways for green methanol synthesis. All these methods were extensively studied in recent decades. CO2 photoreduction offers a renewable and cost-effective alternative using visible light to drive the reaction. Various catalytic systems based on metals [34–37] including noble metals [38–42] have been designed for this application. Interestingly, no studies have to date showed the design of metal-free and dopant-free biomass/waste systems for CO2 photoreduction.

This contribution provides an unprecedented waste-to-wealth concept based on the valorization of orange peel waste into metal-free highly active and stable carbon nanodots for an efficient selective photoreduction of CO2 into green methanol. The findings of this study present a significant breakthrough, illustrating the remarkable potential of these simple and sustainable waste-derived nanomaterials. This discovery holds promise for the simultaneous valorization of waste and the conversion of CO2 into high-value products, with the potential to pave the way into additional waste-to-wealth concepts applied to catalyst design and valuable products generation for a more sustainable future.
All the chemicals were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and used without any specific treatment. The orange peels were collected from Santa Lucia del Mela, Messina, Italy.

CNDs were obtained by performing a microwave-integrated hydrothermal carbonization process (HTC-MW). In a typical HTC-MW test, a mixture of chopped fresh orange peel waste (60 g) and deionized water was ultrasonically stirred for 15 min at room temperature (20 °C) and then transferred to a 300 mL maximum capacity stainless steel autoclave (series 4540 Parr Instrument Company, IL, USA). Residual air was removed from the sealed reactor by repeatedly pressurizing with nitrogen and venting to atmosphere. The reaction mixture was heated up to the temperature of 180 °C with a heating rate of 10 °C.min−1, a stirring speed of 550 rpm and a reaction time fixed at 180 min, under autogenous pressure, continuously monitoring the temperature through a thermocouple placed in the autoclave and connected to the reactor controller inside. After the HTC reaction, the autoclave was rapidly cooled down to room temperature. Then, HTC solid and liquid products were separated by vacuum filtration, with a Buchner funnel and filter paper. Subsequently, the remaining liquid was filtered through 0.1 µm millipore filter paper three times. In order to reduce the size of the suspended particles, the filtrate was placed in a microwave reactor for 5 min at a temperature of 100 °C and autogenous pressure and was subsequently filtered and purified in dialysis bags for 7 days.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed on a Talos F200i microscope. This high-resolution transmission electron microscope is equipped with a Schottky type field emission gun (FEG) with an operating voltage of 200 kV, an energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) microanalysis system, and digital camera. In TEM mode, a resolution between lines of 0.1 nm can be reached at 200 kV. To analyse the sample, several drop of the CNDs solution were deposited on a carbon-coated copper grid. Once dried, the carbon grid was inserted into the equipment on a dedicated sample holder.

Raman analysis was conducted using a Jasco NRS-550 Laser Raman Spectrometer. A laser with a wavelength of 531.94 nm was used with a power 3.6 mW. To ensure that the laser employed was not leading to the degradation of the sample, several acquisitions were performed and similar results were obtained.

FTIR spectrum was obtained on a spectra 3 spectrometer from Perkin Elmer, registered at room temperature in the range between 4000 and 600 cm−1, with scan speed of 0.2 cm/s, resolution of 4.0 cm−1 and accumulation scan of 60.

UV–visible analysis was performed on a lambda 365 spectrophotometer from Perkin Elmer. After a blank experiment in water, the absorption of the CNDs solution was analysed between 200 and 700 nm. The data was acquired at every nanometre with a scan rate of 600 nm/min.

Photoluminescence outcomes were collected by a spectrofluorometer Fluoromax-3 Horiba Jobin–Yvon fluorometer (T = 25 °C) equipped with glass cuvettes (1 cm path length). All the recorded data were developed via OriginPro 2019 software.

PHI Versa Probe II (Physical Electronics), equipped with an Al Kα (1486.6 eV) X-ray source, measured the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra. The survey spectra were recorded with an analyzer energy path of 117 eV, while the C1s, O1s, and N1s, core levels were measured at 23.5 eV passing energy. The X-ray beam size was 100 µm at 25 W. A charge neutralization procedure was performed by simultaneous irradiation of samples using a low-energy electron beam and an ion beam before measuring the spectra. The position of the XPS peaks was referenced to Au metal foil (84.0 eV). XPS peaks were deconvoluted by using the Multipack Data Reduction Software (ULVAC-PHI, Inc), employing a Shirley background curve.

CO2 photoreduction was performed in a glass tube with an internal diameter of 12 mm. To this reactor, 2 mL of the CNDs solution with a concentration of 0.8 mg/mL was added as well as a cross magnetic stirrer. The reactions were performed with a 35 W Flood light lamp with 3300 lm (model: MZT-FL006-DW) with a colour temperature of 5000 K corresponding to a wavelength of approximately 580 nm. The reactor was illuminated for 30 min. Then, the lamp was turned off. Using a gas line composed of a N2 gas bottle, a manometer, a mass flow controller and several valves, nitrogen was bubbled into the CNDs solution with a flow of 50 mL/min for 20 min in order to remove all the air. With a similar gas line, CO2 was bubbled with a flow of 50 mL/min for 20 min to attain the CO2 concentration equilibrium in the reaction media. After these steps, the flow of carbon dioxide remained constant and the lamp was switched on. The reaction time was started at this moment of the procedure. Once the desired reaction time reached, the CO2 flow as well as the lamp were turned off.

A 1 mL sample of the gas phase was injected using a gaslock syringe in a 7890A gas chromatograph (GC) from Agilent Technologies with a packed molecular sieve 5A from Restek. The oven was heated at 80 °C for 5 min and then the temperature increased to 150 °C with a 10 °C/min rate completed with a final plateau of 10 min. The injector temperature was setup to 90 °C. The products were detected with a TCD unit heated at 300 °C. Similarly, a 1 µL sample of the liquid phase was then injected in a different line of the same GC using a RT-Q-bond column with the same oven parameters. The injector temperature was setup to 150 °C. The products were detected with a FID unit heated at 250 °C. To confirm the results, the liquid samples were also injected to another GC (HRGC MEGA 2 series from Carlo Erba Instruments) with a petrocol column. The oven temperature was constant at 250 °C while both the injector and the FID detector were heated at 300 °C.

CNDs were synthesized via microwave-integrated hydrothermal carbonization process (HTC-MW), as described in the experimental part. Briefly, orange peel waste freshly collected from Santa Lucia Del Mela, Messina, Italy, was suspended in deionized water and heated in a stainless-steel autoclave at 180 °C for 180 min. The resulting mixture was then filtered and the obtained material was placed in a microwave reactor at 100 °C for 5 min. This method yielded homogeneous and well-dispersed CNDs. CNDs were characterized using several techniques, including Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), Raman spectroscopy, Fourier Transform Infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), UV–Visible spectroscopy, and Photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL).

The morphology of the CNDs was studied by TEM. Fig. 1 shows spherical shaped CNDs with good dispersion and a uniform average size distribution of about 7.4 ± 2.9 nm. The size histogram was obtained with the measurement of 96 distinct CNDs. In addition to the three micrographs presented herein, twelve micrographs of CNDs with different magnification have been compiled in Fig. S1 to ensure reproducibility (some of the observed carbon nanodots are highlighted with a green circle).

A chemical analysis was performed on this sample by Energy Dispersive X-Rays Spectroscopy (EDS). The obtained elemental composition is represented in Fig. S2. Despite a tiny contamination with Si (0.1 mol%) probably coming from sample preparation for analysis and the presence of Cu (0.8 mol%) due to the copper grid employed for the analysis, only C (98.2 mol%) and O (0.9 mol%) are detected in the carbon dot materials. This analysis confirmed that no residual metal is present in the sample and only pure carbon nanodots are present in solution.

To provide better accuracy on the elemental analysis obtained from EDS, the sample was characterized by XPS. The survey spectra (Fig. 2 D) indicated that the samples contain mainly C, N and O elements (only traces of Si could be additionally observed). The surface composition in weight % is: C1s, 69.61%; N1s, 5.92%; O1s, 22.74%; Si2p 1.73%. As obtained by EDS, the prepared material is mainly composed of carbon. The silicon impurity is still detected, this time in higher quantity. This impurity could come from the preparation of the sample for the XPS analysis. In detail, the solution was dried on a heated thin glass by dropwise addition and the solid phase was recovered by scratching the glass with a spatula. In a logical way, the copper impurity does not appear as present in the carbon-coated grids used for TEM. The main difference is in the quantity of nitrogen and oxygen detected (6% and 23%, respectively).

The fitted C1s spectra of the sample investigated is reported in Fig. 2A. C1s spectra show a more intense asymmetric peak at 284.59 eV (C1 component in blue) which is attributed to CC in graphitic carbon. The peak at 285.96 eV (C2 component in green), is attributed to substitutional CN or CO species in graphitic carbon [43]. The third peak at 287.94 eV (C3 component in red) could be attributed to pyridine-like CN species [43]. N1s core level spectra reported in Fig. 2B show one main peak at 399.8 eV assigned to pyridine-like CN species (N1). [43], in good agreement with the observed C3 component in the C spectrum. O1s spectra reported in Fig. 2C, show two main peaks. The Peak at 531.32 eV (O1 component in red) attributed to CO and the second peak at 532.64 eV (O2 component in green) assigned to C-O species [44].

The intrinsic nanostructure of CNDs was studied using Raman and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, with results presented in Fig. 3 A and B, respectively. CNDs exhibited a strong background signal, likely due to their photoluminescence (PL) properties, with Raman spectra showing two distinct bands at 1357.14 cm−1 and at 1571.42 cm−1, corresponding to the D-band and G-band, respectively. These bands are commonly observed in low-dimensional carbon nanomaterials [45]. The D-band arises from vibrations of carbon atoms with dangling bonds in the disordered graphite termination plane, while the G-band is associated to stretching of sp2-hybridized carbon atoms in a 2D hexagonal lattice [46]. The D-band to G-band intensity ratio (ID/IG) of CNDs was approximately 0.72, pointing to a low content of structural defects in CNDs [47]. Fig. 3B shows the infrared spectrum obtained for carbon nanodots. The wide band located at 3196 cm−1 is associated with the stretching vibration of hydroxyl groups. The peak at 1670 cm−1 could be responsible for the stretching of CC and CO and the sharp peak at 1584 cm−1 is assigned to bending vibration of amine groups probably derived from protein components of the starting orange peel waste [48]. This is coherent with nitrogen content detected by XPS as well as matching well most functionalities present in XPS with respect to those observed in FTIR. Furthermore, the intense signal at 1392 cm−1 could be related to the stretching vibration of the aromatic ring [49]. The small band appeared at 1020 cm−1 corresponds to sp3 hybridized carbon atoms, in good agreement with the D-band observed in Raman results [46,47]. According to literature, the small band at ca. 1060 cm−1 can be associated with vibrations of C-O-C derived from furan compounds (5-HMF and furfural) abundantly present in the aqueous solution of HTC [50–53]. The medium band at 800 cm−1 can be associated to the bending vibration of CC and CH, confirming the presence of aryl and alkyl groups. CNDs spectra clearly showed that carbon nanodots surface possessed relevant functional groups (e.g. amine, hydroxyl and carbonyl groups) that endow CNDs with good water solubility.

PL and UV–Vis analysis are effective techniques to characterize optical properties of novel nanomaterials. The photoluminescent properties of carbon nanodots have been extensively studied, as noted in recent publications [7,54]. The dispersed light-brown suspension emitted strong green light when CNDs were excited at 445 nm (insert Fig. 4 ), with a strong well-defined PL emission band around 548 nm (Fig. 4). These findings indicate that CNDs are fluorescent without any surface passivation treatment.

The photoluminescence behaviour can be potentially explained by the energy difference between the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) in CNDs [55]. The presence of an electron pair on a non-bonding component (n) and an aromatic (π) can promote the HOMO level, leading to energy transfer for n → π* and π → π* transitions, respectively [56]. CNDs may be able to absorb longer wavelengths, which can create localized radiative recombination of electron-hole pairs that release photoluminescence during the de-excitation process. The energy gap, photoluminescence, and quantum yield of CNDs can be altered by the coupling of radicals in such functional groups (e.g., carbonyl and amide groups) with carbons [9].

CNDs exhibited a maximum optical absorption peak in the UV–Vis spectrum at 261 nm, with a slight extension towards the visible region (Fig. 4). The existence of this peak can be attributed to sp2 C-atoms in the π-conjugated system, specifically π-π* transition of double bonded carbon in the aromatic bond CC. The lower energy sub-bands associated with functional groups contribute to the long tail that extends towards the visible region.

To determine the optical band gap energy of the CNDs, the so-called Tauc plot was represented plotting (αhν)1/n versus the photon energy (hν). Here, h is the plank constant (6.626.10−34 J.s−1), ν is the frequency (Hz) and α is the absorbance. Moreover, n can take several values such as 0.5 or 2 for direct allowed transition and indirect allowed transition, respectively. The n = 2 curve is presented in the inset of Fig. 4 (Fig. S3 displays the n = 0.5 curve). The intercept between the linear part of the curve and the abscissa axis reveals optical band gap of the semiconductor. In this case, an optical band gap of 3.6 eV was measured. This band gap corresponds to a light with a wavelength of 345 nm. On the other hand, the direct band gap would be of 4.2 eV corresponding to a light with a wavelength of 260 nm. From these graphical representations, synthesized CNDs possess an allowed indirect band gap. Remarkably, the electron-hole recombination rate is strongly decreased as compared to a semiconductor with an allowed direct band gap. This is due to the change of momentum needed during the recombination of electron-hole pair for an indirect transition. However, the graphical method used on the UV–visible results can lead to important uncertainties. Consequently, the bandgap has been measured by photoluminescence spectroscopy as well. Using this method, a band gap of 2.3 eV was determined. This important difference (3.6 eV vs 2.3 eV) could be ascribed to quantum confinement effect which can lead to the formation of discrete energy levels, sometimes resembling multiple band gaps. The energy gap values documented in the existing literature for this type of material range from 1.91 to 5.77 eV (Table S1). Both values obtained from UV–vis and PL fit into this range. According to the catalytic results obtained with this material, a band gap of 2.3 eV is more coherent than a band gap of 3.6 eV. Effectively, the lamp used for the CO2 photoreduction experiments is a visible lamp with a peak of emission around 550 nm (Fig. S4). While a band gap of 3.6 eV implies the use of UV lamp (with emission around 330 nm), a band gap of 2.3 eV needs a visible lamp with emission around 540 nm such as that successfully employed in this study.

The enhancement observed in the optical properties of our sample could be linked to the existence of nitrogen and oxygen groups within the waste biomass source, which appear to aid in the formation of defects in the carbon core of the nanomaterials, consequently leading in a reduction of bandgap energy. Furthermore, there is a well known correlation between the size, absorption properties and energy gap of carbon nanomaterials, with the optical energy gap decreasing as the size of CDs increases [57,58]. Moreover, according to the quantum confinement theory, the concept suggests that the valence band’s holes and the conduction band’s electrons are confined by the potential barriers present on the carbon dots’ surface [59]. As the particle size decreases, the energy bandgap between the valence and conduction bands widens due to the confinement of electrons and holes [60].

The photocatalytic properties of CNDs were examined by exposing them to a 35 W visible light LED lamp. The gas chromatogram obtained from two different gas chromatography (GC) setups indicate that only one product was formed in the liquid phase during the photoreaction. The injection of methanol probe on the two different GC identified this product as methanol making it the only reaction product. The quantity of methanol formed was determined using a calibration curve (Fig. S5). Moreover, the gas phase of the reaction mixture was also injected to measure the formation of related gases such as carbon monoxide or methane. No other compounds were detected either due to a complete selectivity of the catalyst tested toward methanol or to a formation of gas products with concentration under the GC limit of detection, for which the designed orange peel waste-derived CNDs can be considered fully selective toward methanol production.

Fig. 5 A depicts methanol yield at different reaction times (2, 4, 12, 24, 48, and 72 h) during the photocatalytic conversion of CO2 promoted by CNDs. Logically, methanol quantities increase with reaction time. However, two distinct steps could be observed during these reactions. Until 4 h of reaction, a fast methanol production is detected which slowly progresses after 12 h. Importantly, a remarkably stable methanol production (12.9 µmolMeOH.gcat -1.h−1) is reached after 24 h. Fig. 5B represents the cumulative methanol production rate over 72 h of reaction. The activity clearly increases during the first moments of the reaction until reaching a maximum cumulative methanol production rate of 416.6 µmolMeOH.gcat -1.h−1 (4 h of reaction). The initial increase of methanol productivity between 2 h and 4 h of reaction can be ascribed to the catalyst activation under reactive conditions. After 4 h of reaction, the methanol yield drastically decreased, certainly due to partial degradation of the carbon nanodots either by aggregation of the nanoparticles or by surface chemistry modification. However, despite the initial loss of stability in the first 12 h, the metal-free catalyst can be considered as stable under optimum reactions conditions after 24 h of reaction, another unprecedented result from literature.

Table S2 is compiling the methanol production performances of various published materials. It is appearing the design CNDs is among the best performing reported photocatalysts. The absence of any metal, co-catalyst or dopant as well as the waste-derived origin makes CNDs a promising candidate for the photoreduction of CO2 into methanol.

A sequence of blank trials was conducted to examine the existence of methanol in solution, with results are compiled in Table S3. As discussed previously, test 1 combining CO2 flow, light and the carbon nanodots exhibit a remarkable methanol production of 1666.4 µmol.g−1 after 4 h of reaction. However, the same experiment conducted with a nitrogen flow instead of CO2 (test 2) led to inexistent methanol formation. These findings demonstrate that methanol is coming from CO2 reduction. Test 3 was performed with CNDs and CO2 flow but without any illumination, again leading to no methanol formation, confirming the photocatalytic aspect of the reaction. Finally, test 4 was carried out with CO2 flow and light source but without any CNDs. The complete absence of methanol for this test is proving the essential role and activity of CNDs. In summary, these results indicate that the absence of photocatalyst, light or CO2 prevented any methanol production. These findings highlight the presence of a photocatalytic reaction and that the observed methanol is coming from the reduction of carbon dioxide. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first example of the successful utilization of a metal-free, co-catalyst/dopant free waste-derived CNDs photocatalyst in CO2 photoreduction towards methanol.

From a mechanistic point of view, despite the increasing interest of CNDs for photocatalytic applications, the detailed mechanism occurring on CNDs alone is still not well established. According to literature, several pathways can exist with different reaction intermediate including formaldehyde, carbene, formyl or glyoxal [61]. Since no carbon monoxide is detected, the latest can be excluded for CNDs. The three other pathways are plausible for the prepared materials. Fig. 6 provides a schematic overview of the mechanism of photocatalytic reduction of CO2 by the synthesized CNDs. First, a photon with an energy superior or equal to 2.3 eV (wavelength superior or equal to 345 nm) is absorbed by CNDs. Then, one electron from the valence band is exited to the conduction band of this semiconductor material leading to the formation of a hole in the valence band. CO2 is adsorbed on CNDs surface and reduced into methanol with an electron from the conduction band. On the other hand, water is oxidised into oxygen with one hole from the valence band.

Fast electron-hole recombination could be problematic for single photocatalyst system. In this contribution, the high performance of the prepared waste-derived, metal-free carbon nanodots pointed to small-to-none fast electron-hole recombination. As mentioned previously, these type of materials can exhibit quantum confinement effects that could explain the relative stability of the electron-hole. In this study, no strategies were employed to further improve the lifetime of the carriers. However, the lost of activity after 4 h of reaction could come from a reduction of quantum confinement effect led by carbon nanodots agglomeration or partial degradation. Further investigations will be performed to avoid these phenomena, aiming to enhance the stability and activity of the designed CNDs.

Metal-free orange peel waste-derived CNDs were synthesized via a hydrothermal–microwave integrated approach employing waste materials as a carbon source. CNDs exhibited intrinsic photo-activity, with remarkable results in the photocatalytic reduction of CO2 into methanol. For the first time, CNDs without any metal/nanoparticles or co-catalyst has been reported for this reaction. A maximum methanol production of 416.6 µmolMeOH.gcat -1.h−1 was achieved, with a highly stable methanol production of 12.9 µmolMeOH.gcat -1.h−1 measured after 72 h, remaining among the highest reported methanol production via photocatalytic CO2 conversion in literature. Therefore, this study underscores the effective waste-to-fuel strategy starting from orange peel waste to design CNDs that can reduce the CO2 into a valuable compound in order to both tackle the environmental issues and need of green methanol synthesis alternative.

Viviana Bressi: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Thomas Len: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Salvatore Abate: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Claudia Espro: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Rafael Luque: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: VB would like to express her gratitude to the University of Messina and the University of Cordoba for the opportunity to collaborate, as well as to Marzia for her unwaveringsupport. TL gratefully acknowledges Ministerio de Ciencia e Innovacion for the provision of a Juan de la Cierva Fellowship (Ref: FJC2021-047151-I).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2024.149989.

ce:section.ce:para:
